Agatamori was later appointed {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}} and subsequently became one of the first {{ill|Inspectors (Ancient Japan)|ja|按察使}}, overseeing the provinces of {{ill|Sagami|ja|相模}}, {{ill|Kōzuke|ja|上野}}, and {{ill|Shimotsuke|ja|下野}}. He also served as {{ill|Governor of Harima|ja|播磨守}}.
In {{ill|Yōrō 4|ja|養老4年}} (720), he was appointed {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}} to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, following the assassination of {{ill|Kamu Yamakano|ja|上毛野広人}}, the Inspector of Mutsu Province.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 45–46]</ref> His campaign achieved partial success, and he was promoted to {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位上}} upon his return.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 44]</ref>
=== Role in Court Politics ===
In {{ill|Shinki 6|ja|神亀6年}} (729), Agatamori was appointed as a provisional {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} during the {{ill|Conspiracy of Prince Nagaya|ja|長屋王の変}} and became a court noble shortly afterward, holding key positions under the {{ill|Fujiwara no Four Brothers|ja|藤原四兄弟}} regime. He reached the rank of {{ill|Shōsan-i|ja|正三位}} and served as {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言}}.
In {{ill|Tenpyō 7|ja|天平7年}} (735), he rejected a delegation from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} that had unilaterally changed its national name.<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 7, 2/27</ref>
Agatamori died in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at age 70, likely due to the smallpox epidemic that claimed many lives, including the Fujiwara brothers.<ref name="naoki" />
== Family ==
* Father: {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 9, 6/23</ref> * Children: * Daughter: Wife of {{ill|Fujiwara no Hamanari|ja|藤原浜成}}<ref>‘Sonpi Bunmyaku’</ref> * Daughter: Possibly {{ill|Tanbe no Iratsume|ja|丹比郎女}}, wife of {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and mother of {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}<ref>Kawakami [1971]</ref>

revision.text:
'''Tajihi no Agatamori''' (多治比縣守) was a court noble and government official during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. He was a son of {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}, who served as a {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}} (Minister of the Left). Agatamori played key roles in diplomacy, governance, and military affairs, including serving as {{ill|Envoy to Tang China|ja|遣唐使押使}}, {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}}, and {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}}.
== Biography ==
Agatamori was born in the 7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}'s reign (668), as recorded in sources like the {{ill|Kugyō Bōnin|ja|公卿補任}}, which states he passed away in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at the age of 70.<ref name="naoki">Naoki [2006: 617]</ref> His family, the {{ill|Tajihi clan|ja|多治比氏}}, descended from {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, making them one of the prominent noble lineages of the time.<ref>Takashima [1969: 10–11]</ref>
=== Mission to Tang China ===
In {{ill|Keiun 2|ja|慶雲2年}} (705), Agatamori was promoted from {{ill|Jūroku-i|ja|従六位}} to {{ill|Jūgo-i|ja|従五位下}}. His career advanced rapidly under the reign of {{ill|Empress Genmei|ja|元明天皇}}, and by {{ill|Wadō 8|ja|和銅8年}} (715), he had reached {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位下}} and was appointed {{ill|Zōkyūkyō|ja|造宮卿}}, overseeing the construction of {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}}.
In {{ill|Reiki 2|ja|霊亀2年}} (716), Agatamori was appointed Envoy to Tang China (遣唐押使). He departed for China in {{ill|Reiki 3|ja|霊亀3年}} (717), leading a large delegation of 557 members, including notable figures such as {{ill|Abe no Nakamaro|ja|阿倍仲麻呂}}, {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}, and {{ill|Gembō|ja|玄昉}}. The delegation reached {{ill|Chang'an|ja|長安}} in October and returned safely in {{ill|Yōrō 2|ja|養老2年}} (718). Agatamori received further promotions upon his return.<ref>Ueda [2006: 80–83]</ref>
=== Governor and Regional Commander ===
Agatamori was later appointed {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}} and subsequently became one of the first {{ill|Inspectors (Ancient Japan)|ja|按察使}}, overseeing the provinces of {{ill|Sagami|ja|相模}}, {{ill|Kōzuke|ja|上野}}, and {{ill|Shimotsuke|ja|下野}}. He also served as {{ill|Governor of Harima|ja|播磨守}}.
In {{ill|Yōrō 4|ja|養老4年}} (720), he was appointed {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}} to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, following the assassination of {{ill|Kamu Yamakano|ja|上毛野広人}}, the Inspector of Mutsu Province.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 45–46]</ref> His campaign achieved partial success, and he was promoted to {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位上}} upon his return.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 44]</ref>
=== Role in Court Politics ===
In {{ill|Shinki 6|ja|神亀6年}} (729), Agatamori was appointed as a provisional {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} during the {{ill|Conspiracy of Prince Nagaya|ja|長屋王の変}} and became a court noble shortly afterward, holding key positions under the {{ill|Fujiwara no Four Brothers|ja|藤原四兄弟}} regime. He reached the rank of {{ill|Shōsan-i|ja|正三位}} and served as {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言}}.
In {{ill|Tenpyō 7|ja|天平7年}} (735), he rejected a delegation from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} that had unilaterally changed its national name.<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 7, 2/27</ref>
Agatamori died in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at age 70, likely due to the smallpox epidemic that claimed many lives, including the Fujiwara brothers.<ref name="naoki" />
== Family ==
* Father: {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 9, 6/23</ref> * Children: * Daughter: Wife of {{ill|Fujiwara no Hamanari|ja|藤原浜成}}<ref>‘Sonpi Bunmyaku’</ref> * Daughter: Possibly {{ill|Tanbe no Iratsume|ja|丹比郎女}}, wife of {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and mother of {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}<ref>Kawakami [1971]</ref>
== References ==
{{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography ==
* Ueda, Yū. *{{ill|遣唐使全航海|ja}}*. Sōshisha, 2006. ISBN 978-4794215444. * Suzuki, Takuya. *{{ill|Emishi to Tōhoku Sensō|ja|蝦夷と東北戦争}}*. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2008. ISBN 978-4642063135. * Naoki, Kōjirō. *Nihon Kodai Chūsei Jinmei Jiten*. Yoshikawa Kōbu

In {{ill|Shinki 6|ja|神亀6年}} (729), Agatamori was appointed as a provisional {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} during the {{ill|Conspiracy of Prince Nagaya|ja|長屋王の変}} and became a court noble shortly afterward, holding key positions under the {{ill|Fujiwara no Four Brothers|ja|藤原四兄弟}} regime. He reached the rank of {{ill|Shōsan-i|ja|正三位}} and served as {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言}}.
In {{ill|Tenpyō 7|ja|天平7年}} (735), he rejected a delegation from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} that had unilaterally changed its national name.<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 7, 2/27</ref>
Agatamori died in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at age 70, likely due to the smallpox epidemic that claimed many lives, including the Fujiwara brothers.<ref name="naoki" />
== Family ==
* Father: {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 9, 6/23</ref> * Children: * Daughter: Wife of {{ill|Fujiwara no Hamanari|ja|藤原浜成}}<ref>‘Sonpi Bunmyaku’</ref> * Daughter: Possibly {{ill|Tanbe no Iratsume|ja|丹比郎女}}, wife of {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and mother of {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}<ref>Kawakami [1971]</ref>

{{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography ==
* Ueda, Yū. *{{ill|遣唐使全航海|ja}}*. Sōshisha, 2006. ISBN 978-4794215444. * Suzuki, Takuya. *{{ill|Emishi to Tōhoku Sensō|ja|蝦夷と東北戦争}}*. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2008. ISBN 978-4642063135. * Naoki, Kōjirō. *Nihon Kodai Chūsei Jinmei Jiten*. Yoshikawa Kōbu

'''Tajihi no Agatamori''' (多治比縣守) was a court noble and government official during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. He was a son of {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}, who served as a {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}} (Minister of the Left). Agatamori played key roles in diplomacy, governance, and military affairs, including serving as {{ill|Envoy to Tang China|ja|遣唐使押使}}, {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}}, and {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}}.
== Biography ==
Agatamori was born in the 7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}'s reign (668), as recorded in sources like the {{ill|Kugyō Bōnin|ja|公卿補任}}, which states he passed away in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at the age of 70.<ref name="naoki">Naoki [2006: 617]</ref> His family, the {{ill|Tajihi clan|ja|多治比氏}}, descended from {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, making them one of the prominent noble lineages of the time.<ref>Takashima [1969: 10–11]</ref>
=== Mission to Tang China ===
In {{ill|Keiun 2|ja|慶雲2年}} (705), Agatamori was promoted from {{ill|Jūroku-i|ja|従六位}} to {{ill|Jūgo-i|ja|従五位下}}. His career advanced rapidly under the reign of {{ill|Empress Genmei|ja|元明天皇}}, and by {{ill|Wadō 8|ja|和銅8年}} (715), he had reached {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位下}} and was appointed {{ill|Zōkyūkyō|ja|造宮卿}}, overseeing the construction of {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}}.
In {{ill|Reiki 2|ja|霊亀2年}} (716), Agatamori was appointed Envoy to Tang China (遣唐押使). He departed for China in {{ill|Reiki 3|ja|霊亀3年}} (717), leading a large delegation of 557 members, including notable figures such as {{ill|Abe no Nakamaro|ja|阿倍仲麻呂}}, {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}, and {{ill|Gembō|ja|玄昉}}. The delegation reached {{ill|Chang'an|ja|長安}} in October and returned safely in {{ill|Yōrō 2|ja|養老2年}} (718). Agatamori received further promotions upon his return.<ref>Ueda [2006: 80–83]</ref>
=== Governor and Regional Commander ===
Agatamori was later appointed {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}} and subsequently became one of the first {{ill|Inspectors (Ancient Japan)|ja|按察使}}, overseeing the provinces of {{ill|Sagami|ja|相模}}, {{ill|Kōzuke|ja|上野}}, and {{ill|Shimotsuke|ja|下野}}. He also served as {{ill|Governor of Harima|ja|播磨守}}.
In {{ill|Yōrō 4|ja|養老4年}} (720), he was appointed {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}} to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, following the assassination of {{ill|Kamu Yamakano|ja|上毛野広人}}, the Inspector of Mutsu Province.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 45–46]</ref> His campaign achieved partial success, and he was promoted to {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位上}} upon his return.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 44]</ref>
=== Role in Court Politics ===
In {{ill|Shinki 6|ja|神亀6年}} (729), Agatamori was appointed as a provisional {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} during the {{ill|Conspiracy of Prince Nagaya|ja|長屋王の変}} and became a court noble shortly afterward, holding key positions under the {{ill|Fujiwara no Four Brothers|ja|藤原四兄弟}} regime. He reached the rank of {{ill|Shōsan-i|ja|正三位}} and served as {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言}}.
In {{ill|Tenpyō 7|ja|天平7年}} (735), he rejected a delegation from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} that had unilaterally changed its national name.<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 7, 2/27</ref>
Agatamori died in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at age 70, likely due to the smallpox epidemic that claimed many lives, including the Fujiwara brothers.<ref name="naoki" />
== Family ==
* Father: {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 9, 6/23</ref> * Children: * Daughter: Wife of {{ill|Fujiwara no Hamanari|ja|藤原浜成}}<ref>‘Sonpi Bunmyaku’</ref> * Daughter: Possibly {{ill|Tanbe no Iratsume|ja|丹比郎女}}, wife of {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and mother of {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}<ref>Kawakami [1971]</ref>
== References ==
{{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography ==
* Ueda, Yū. *{{ill|遣唐使全航海|ja}}*. Sōshisha, 2006. ISBN 978-4794215444. * Suzuki, Takuya. *{{ill|Emishi to Tōhoku Sensō|ja|蝦夷と東北戦争}}*. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2008. ISBN 978-4642063135. * Naoki, Kōjirō. *Nihon Kodai Chūsei Jinmei Jiten*. Yoshikawa Kōbu

'''Tajihi no Agatamori''' (多治比縣守) was a court noble and government official during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. He was a son of {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}, who served as a {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}} (Minister of the Left). Agatamori played key roles in diplomacy, governance, and military affairs, including serving as {{ill|Envoy to Tang China|ja|遣唐使押使}}, {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}}, and {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}}.
== Biography ==
Agatamori was born in the 7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}'s reign (668), as recorded in sources like the {{ill|Kugyō Bōnin|ja|公卿補任}}, which states he passed away in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at the age of 70.<ref name="naoki">Naoki [2006: 617]</ref> His family, the {{ill|Tajihi clan|ja|多治比氏}}, descended from {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, making them one of the prominent noble lineages of the time.<ref>Takashima [1969: 10–11]</ref>
=== Mission to Tang China ===
In {{ill|Keiun 2|ja|慶雲2年}} (705), Agatamori was promoted from {{ill|Jūroku-i|ja|従六位}} to {{ill|Jūgo-i|ja|従五位下}}. His career advanced rapidly under the reign of {{ill|Empress Genmei|ja|元明天皇}}, and by {{ill|Wadō 8|ja|和銅8年}} (715), he had reached {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位下}} and was appointed {{ill|Zōkyūkyō|ja|造宮卿}}, overseeing the construction of {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}}.
In {{ill|Reiki 2|ja|霊亀2年}} (716), Agatamori was appointed Envoy to Tang China (遣唐押使). He departed for China in {{ill|Reiki 3|ja|霊亀3年}} (717), leading a large delegation of 557 members, including notable figures such as {{ill|Abe no Nakamaro|ja|阿倍仲麻呂}}, {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}, and {{ill|Gembō|ja|玄昉}}. The delegation reached {{ill|Chang'an|ja|長安}} in October and returned safely in {{ill|Yōrō 2|ja|養老2年}} (718). Agatamori received further promotions upon his return.<ref>Ueda [2006: 80–83]</ref>
=== Governor and Regional Commander ===
Agatamori was later appointed {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}} and subsequently became one of the first {{ill|Inspectors (Ancient Japan)|ja|按察使}}, overseeing the provinces of {{ill|Sagami|ja|相模}}, {{ill|Kōzuke|ja|上野}}, and {{ill|Shimotsuke|ja|下野}}. He also served as {{ill|Governor of Harima|ja|播磨守}}.
In {{ill|Yōrō 4|ja|養老4年}} (720), he was appointed {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}} to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, following the assassination of {{ill|Kamu Yamakano|ja|上毛野広人}}, the Inspector of Mutsu Province.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 45–46]</ref> His campaign achieved partial success, and he was promoted to {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位上}} upon his return.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 44]</ref>
=== Role in Court Politics ===
In {{ill|Shinki 6|ja|神亀6年}} (729), Agatamori was appointed as a provisional {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} during the {{ill|Conspiracy of Prince Nagaya|ja|長屋王の変}} and became a court noble shortly afterward, holding key positions under the {{ill|Fujiwara no Four Brothers|ja|藤原四兄弟}} regime. He reached the rank of {{ill|Shōsan-i|ja|正三位}} and served as {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言}}.
In {{ill|Tenpyō 7|ja|天平7年}} (735), he rejected a delegation from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} that had unilaterally changed its national name.<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 7, 2/27</ref>
Agatamori died in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at age 70, likely due to the smallpox epidemic that claimed many lives, including the Fujiwara brothers.<ref name="naoki" />
== Family ==
* Father: {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 9, 6/23</ref> * Children: ** Daughter: Wife of {{ill|Fujiwara no Hamanari|ja|藤原浜成}}<ref>‘Sonpi Bunmyaku’</ref> ** Daughter: Possibly {{ill|Tanbe no Iratsume|ja|丹比郎女}}, wife of {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and mother of {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}<ref>Kawakami [1971]</ref>
== References ==
{{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography ==
* Ueda, Yū. *{{ill|遣唐使全航海|ja}}*. Sōshisha, 2006. ISBN 978-4794215444. * Suzuki, Takuya. *{{ill|Emishi to Tōhoku Sensō|ja|蝦夷と東北戦争}}*. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2008. ISBN 978-4642063135. * Naoki, Kōjirō. *Nihon Kodai Chūsei Jinmei Jiten*. Yoshikawa Kōbu

The delegation reached {{ill|Chang'an|ja|長安}} in October and returned safely in {{ill|Yōrō 2|ja|養老2年}} (718). Agatamori received further promotions upon his return.<ref>Ueda [2006: 80–83]</ref>
=== Governor and Regional Commander ===
Agatamori was later appointed {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}} and subsequently became one of the first {{ill|Inspectors (Ancient Japan)|ja|按察使}}, overseeing the provinces of {{ill|Sagami|ja|相模}}, {{ill|Kōzuke|ja|上野}}, and {{ill|Shimotsuke|ja|下野}}. He also served as {{ill|Governor of Harima|ja|播磨守}}.
In {{ill|Yōrō 4|ja|養老4年}} (720), he was appointed {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}} to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, following the assassination of {{ill|Kamu Yamakano|ja|上毛野広人}}, the Inspector of Mutsu Province.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 45–46]</ref> His campaign achieved partial success, and he was promoted to {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位上}} upon his return.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 44]</ref>

In {{ill|Shinki 6|ja|神亀6年}} (729), Agatamori was appointed as a provisional {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} during the {{ill|Conspiracy of Prince Nagaya|ja|長屋王の変}} and became a court noble shortly afterward, holding key positions under the {{ill|Fujiwara no Four Brothers|ja|藤原四兄弟}} regime. He reached the rank of {{ill|Shōsan-i|ja|正三位}} and served as {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言}}.
In {{ill|Tenpyō 7|ja|天平7年}} (735), he rejected a delegation from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} that had unilaterally changed its national name.<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 7, 2/27</ref>
Agatamori died in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at age 70, likely due to the smallpox epidemic that claimed many lives, including the Fujiwara brothers.<ref name="naoki" />

revision.text:
'''Tajihi no Agatamori''' (多治比縣守) was a court noble and government official during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. He was a son of {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}, who served as a {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}} (Minister of the Left). Agatamori played key roles in diplomacy, governance, and military affairs, including serving as {{ill|Envoy to Tang China|ja|遣唐使押使}}, {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}}, and {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}}.
== Biography ==
Agatamori was born in the 7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}'s reign (668), as recorded in sources like the {{ill|Kugyō Bōnin|ja|公卿補任}}, which states he passed away in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at the age of 70.<ref name="naoki">Naoki [2006: 617]</ref> His family, the {{ill|Tajihi clan|ja|多治比氏}}, descended from {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, making them one of the prominent noble lineages of the time.<ref>Takashima [1969: 10–11]</ref>
=== Mission to Tang China ===
In {{ill|Keiun 2|ja|慶雲2年}} (705), Agatamori was promoted from {{ill|Jūroku-i|ja|従六位}} to {{ill|Jūgo-i|ja|従五位下}}. His career advanced rapidly under the reign of {{ill|Empress Genmei|ja|元明天皇}}, and by {{ill|Wadō 8|ja|和銅8年}} (715), he had reached {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位下}} and was appointed {{ill|Zōkyūkyō|ja|造宮卿}}, overseeing the construction of {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}}.
In {{ill|Reiki 2|ja|霊亀2年}} (716), Agatamori was appointed Envoy to Tang China (遣唐押使). He departed for China in {{ill|Reiki 3|ja|霊亀3年}} (717), leading a large delegation of 557 members, including notable figures such as {{ill|Abe no Nakamaro|ja|阿倍仲麻呂}}, {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}, and {{ill|Gembō|ja|玄昉}}. The delegation reached {{ill|Chang'an|ja|長安}} in October and returned safely in {{ill|Yōrō 2|ja|養老2年}} (718). Agatamori received further promotions upon his return.<ref>Ueda [2006: 80–83]</ref>
=== Governor and Regional Commander ===
Agatamori was later appointed {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}} and subsequently became one of the first {{ill|Inspectors (Ancient Japan)|ja|按察使}}, overseeing the provinces of {{ill|Sagami|ja|相模}}, {{ill|Kōzuke|ja|上野}}, and {{ill|Shimotsuke|ja|下野}}. He also served as {{ill|Governor of Harima|ja|播磨守}}.
In {{ill|Yōrō 4|ja|養老4年}} (720), he was appointed {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}} to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, following the assassination of {{ill|Kamu Yamakano|ja|上毛野広人}}, the Inspector of Mutsu Province.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 45–46]</ref> His campaign achieved partial success, and he was promoted to {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位上}} upon his return.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 44]</ref>
=== Role in Court Politics ===
In {{ill|Shinki 6|ja|神亀6年}} (729), Agatamori was appointed as a provisional {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} during the {{ill|Conspiracy of Prince Nagaya|ja|長屋王の変}} and became a court noble shortly afterward, holding key positions under the {{ill|Fujiwara no Four Brothers|ja|藤原四兄弟}} regime. He reached the rank of {{ill|Shōsan-i|ja|正三位}} and served as {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言}}.
In {{ill|Tenpyō 7|ja|天平7年}} (735), he rejected a delegation from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} that had unilaterally changed its national name.<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 7, 2/27</ref>
Agatamori died in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at age 70, likely due to the smallpox epidemic that claimed many lives, including the Fujiwara brothers.<ref name="naoki" />
== Family ==
* Father: {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 9, 6/23</ref> * Children: ** Daughter: Wife of {{ill|Fujiwara no Hamanari|ja|藤原浜成}}<ref>‘Sonpi Bunmyaku’</ref> ** Daughter: Possibly {{ill|Tanbe no Iratsume|ja|丹比郎女}}, wife of {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and mother of {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}<ref>Kawakami [1971]</ref>
== References ==
{{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography ==
* Ueda, Yū. *{{ill|遣唐使全航海|ja}}*. Sōshisha, 2006. ISBN 978-4794215444. * Suzuki, Takuya. *{{ill|Emishi to Tōhoku Sensō|ja|蝦夷と東北戦争}}*. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2008. ISBN 978-4642063135. * Naoki, Kōjirō. *Nihon Kodai Chūsei Jinmei Jiten*. Yoshikawa Kōbu

* Father: {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 9, 6/23</ref> * Children: ** Daughter: Wife of {{ill|Fujiwara no Hamanari|ja|藤原浜成}}<ref>‘Sonpi Bunmyaku’</ref> ** Daughter: Possibly {{ill|Tanbe no Iratsume|ja|丹比郎女}}, wife of {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and mother of {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}<ref>Kawakami [1971]</ref>

revision.text:
'''Tajihi no Agatamori''' (多治比縣守) was a court noble and government official during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. He was a son of {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}, who served as a {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}} (Minister of the Left). Agatamori played key roles in diplomacy, governance, and military affairs, including serving as {{ill|Envoy to Tang China|ja|遣唐使押使}}, {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}}, and {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}}.
== Biography ==
Agatamori was born in the 7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}'s reign (668), as recorded in sources like the {{ill|Kugyō Bōnin|ja|公卿補任}}, which states he passed away in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at the age of 70.<ref name="naoki">Naoki [2006: 617]</ref> His family, the {{ill|Tajihi clan|ja|多治比氏}}, descended from {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, making them one of the prominent noble lineages of the time.<ref>Takashima [1969: 10–11]</ref>
=== Mission to Tang China ===
In {{ill|Keiun 2|ja|慶雲2年}} (705), Agatamori was promoted from {{ill|Jūroku-i|ja|従六位}} to {{ill|Jūgo-i|ja|従五位下}}. His career advanced rapidly under the reign of {{ill|Empress Genmei|ja|元明天皇}}, and by {{ill|Wadō 8|ja|和銅8年}} (715), he had reached {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位下}} and was appointed {{ill|Zōkyūkyō|ja|造宮卿}}, overseeing the construction of {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}}.
In {{ill|Reiki 2|ja|霊亀2年}} (716), Agatamori was appointed Envoy to Tang China (遣唐押使). He departed for China in {{ill|Reiki 3|ja|霊亀3年}} (717), leading a large delegation of 557 members, including notable figures such as {{ill|Abe no Nakamaro|ja|阿倍仲麻呂}}, {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}, and {{ill|Gembō|ja|玄昉}}. The delegation reached {{ill|Chang'an|ja|長安}} in October and returned safely in {{ill|Yōrō 2|ja|養老2年}} (718). Agatamori received further promotions upon his return.<ref>Ueda [2006: 80–83]</ref>
=== Governor and Regional Commander ===
Agatamori was later appointed {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}} and subsequently became one of the first {{ill|Inspectors (Ancient Japan)|ja|按察使}}, overseeing the provinces of {{ill|Sagami|ja|相模}}, {{ill|Kōzuke|ja|上野}}, and {{ill|Shimotsuke|ja|下野}}. He also served as {{ill|Governor of Harima|ja|播磨守}}.
In {{ill|Yōrō 4|ja|養老4年}} (720), he was appointed {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}} to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, following the assassination of {{ill|Kamu Yamakano|ja|上毛野広人}}, the Inspector of Mutsu Province.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 45–46]</ref> His campaign achieved partial success, and he was promoted to {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位上}} upon his return.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 44]</ref>
=== Role in Court Politics ===
In {{ill|Shinki 6|ja|神亀6年}} (729), Agatamori was appointed as a provisional {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} during the {{ill|Conspiracy of Prince Nagaya|ja|長屋王の変}} and became a court noble shortly afterward, holding key positions under the {{ill|Fujiwara no Four Brothers|ja|藤原四兄弟}} regime. He reached the rank of {{ill|Shōsan-i|ja|正三位}} and served as {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言}}.
In {{ill|Tenpyō 7|ja|天平7年}} (735), he rejected a delegation from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} that had unilaterally changed its national name.<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 7, 2/27</ref>
Agatamori died in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at age 70, likely due to the smallpox epidemic that claimed many lives, including the Fujiwara brothers.<ref name="naoki" />
== Family ==
* Father: {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 9, 6/23</ref> * Children: ** Daughter: Wife of {{ill|Fujiwara no Hamanari|ja|藤原浜成}}<ref>‘Sonpi Bunmyaku’</ref> ** Daughter: Possibly {{ill|Tanbe no Iratsume|ja|丹比郎女}}, wife of {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and mother of {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}<ref>Kawakami [1971]</ref>
== References ==
{{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography ==
* Ueda, Yū. *{{ill|遣唐使全航海|ja}}*. Sōshisha, 2006. ISBN 978-4794215444. * Suzuki, Takuya. *{{ill|Emishi to Tōhoku Sensō|ja|蝦夷と東北戦争}}*. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2008. ISBN 978-4642063135. * Naoki, Kōjirō. *Nihon Kodai Chūsei Jinmei Jiten*. Yoshikawa Kōbu

== Biography ==
Agatamori was born in the 7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}'s reign (668), as recorded in sources like the {{ill|Kugyō Bōnin|ja|公卿補任}}, which states he passed away in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at the age of 70.<ref name="naoki">Naoki [2006: 617]</ref> His family, the {{ill|Tajihi clan|ja|多治比氏}}, descended from {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, making them one of the prominent noble lineages of the time.<ref>Takashima [1969: 10–11]</ref>
=== Mission to Tang China ===
In {{ill|Keiun 2|ja|慶雲2年}} (705), Agatamori was promoted from {{ill|Jūroku-i|ja|従六位}} to {{ill|Jūgo-i|ja|従五位下}}. His career advanced rapidly under the reign of {{ill|Empress Genmei|ja|元明天皇}}, and by {{ill|Wadō 8|ja|和銅8年}} (715), he had reached {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位下}} and was appointed {{ill|Zōkyūkyō|ja|造宮卿}}, overseeing the construction of {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}}.
In {{ill|Reiki 2|ja|霊亀2年}} (716), Agatamori was appointed Envoy to Tang China (遣唐押使). He departed for China in {{ill|Reiki 3|ja|霊亀3年}} (717), leading a large delegation of 557 members, including notable figures such as {{ill|Abe no Nakamaro|ja|阿倍仲麻呂}}, {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}, and {{ill|Gembō|ja|玄昉}}. The delegation reached {{ill|Chang'an|ja|長安}} in October and returned safely in {{ill|Yōrō 2|ja|養老2年}} (718). Agatamori received further promotions upon his return.<ref>Ueda [2006: 80–83]</ref>
=== Governor and Regional Commander ===
Agatamori was later appointed {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}} and subsequently became one of the first {{ill|Inspectors (Ancient Japan)|ja|按察使}}, overseeing the provinces of {{ill|Sagami|ja|相模}}, {{ill|Kōzuke|ja|上野}}, and {{ill|Shimotsuke|ja|下野}}. He also served as {{ill|Governor of Harima|ja|播磨守}}.
In {{ill|Yōrō 4|ja|養老4年}} (720), he was appointed {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}} to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, following the assassination of {{ill|Kamu Yamakano|ja|上毛野広人}}, the Inspector of Mutsu Province.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 45–46]</ref> His campaign achieved partial success, and he was promoted to {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位上}} upon his return.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 44]</ref>
=== Role in Court Politics ===
In {{ill|Shinki 6|ja|神亀6年}} (729), Agatamori was appointed as a provisional {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} during the {{ill|Conspiracy of Prince Nagaya|ja|長屋王の変}} and became a court noble shortly afterward, holding key positions under the {{ill|Fujiwara no Four Brothers|ja|藤原四兄弟}} regime. He reached the rank of {{ill|Shōsan-i|ja|正三位}} and served as {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言}}.
In {{ill|Tenpyō 7|ja|天平7年}} (735), he rejected a delegation from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} that had unilaterally changed its national name.<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 7, 2/27</ref>
Agatamori died in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at age 70, likely due to the smallpox epidemic that claimed many lives, including the Fujiwara brothers.<ref name="naoki" />
== Family ==
* Father: {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 9, 6/23</ref> * Children: ** Daughter: Wife of {{ill|Fujiwara no Hamanari|ja|藤原浜成}}<ref>‘Sonpi Bunmyaku’</ref> ** Daughter: Possibly {{ill|Tanbe no Iratsume|ja|丹比郎女}}, wife of {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and mother of {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}<ref>Kawakami [1971]</ref>
== References ==

== Bibliography ==
* Ueda, Yū. *{{ill|遣唐使全航海|ja}}*. Sōshisha, 2006. ISBN 978-4794215444. * Suzuki, Takuya. *{{ill|Emishi to Tōhoku Sensō|ja|蝦夷と東北戦争}}*. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2008. ISBN 978-4642063135. * Naoki, Kōjirō. *Nihon Kodai Chūsei Jinmei Jiten*. Yoshikawa Kōbu

He departed for China in {{ill|Reiki 3|ja|霊亀3年}} (717), leading a large delegation of 557 members, including notable figures such as {{ill|Abe no Nakamaro|ja|阿倍仲麻呂}}, {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}, and {{ill|Gembō|ja|玄昉}}. The delegation reached {{ill|Chang'an|ja|長安}} in October and returned safely in {{ill|Yōrō 2|ja|養老2年}} (718). Agatamori received further promotions upon his return.<ref>Ueda [2006: 80–83]</ref>
=== Governor and Regional Commander ===
Agatamori was later appointed {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}} and subsequently became one of the first {{ill|Inspectors (Ancient Japan)|ja|按察使}}, overseeing the provinces of {{ill|Sagami|ja|相模}}, {{ill|Kōzuke|ja|上野}}, and {{ill|Shimotsuke|ja|下野}}. He also served as {{ill|Governor of Harima|ja|播磨守}}.
In {{ill|Yōrō 4|ja|養老4年}} (720), he was appointed {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}} to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, following the assassination of {{ill|Kamu Yamakano|ja|上毛野広人}}, the Inspector of Mutsu Province.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 45–46]</ref> His campaign achieved partial success, and he was promoted to {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位上}} upon his return.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 44]</ref>
=== Role in Court Politics ===
In {{ill|Shinki 6|ja|神亀6年}} (729), Agatamori was appointed as a provisional {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} during the {{ill|Conspiracy of Prince Nagaya|ja|長屋王の変}} and became a court noble shortly afterward, holding key positions under the {{ill|Fujiwara no Four Brothers|ja|藤原四兄弟}} regime. He reached the rank of {{ill|Shōsan-i|ja|正三位}} and served as {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言}}.
In {{ill|Tenpyō 7|ja|天平7年}} (735), he rejected a delegation from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} that had unilaterally changed its national name.<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 7, 2/27</ref>
Agatamori died in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at age 70, likely due to the smallpox epidemic that claimed many lives, including the Fujiwara brothers.<ref name="naoki" />
== Family ==
* Father: {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 9, 6/23</ref> * Children: ** Daughter: Wife of {{ill|Fujiwara no Hamanari|ja|藤原浜成}}<ref>‘Sonpi Bunmyaku’</ref> ** Daughter: Possibly {{ill|Tanbe no Iratsume|ja|丹比郎女}}, wife of {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and mother of {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}<ref>Kawakami [1971]</ref>

'''Tajihi no Agatamori''' (多治比縣守) was a court noble and government official during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. He was a son of {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}, who served as a {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}} (Minister of the Left). Agatamori played key roles in diplomacy, governance, and military affairs, including serving as {{ill|Envoy to Tang China|ja|遣唐使押使}}, {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}}, and {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}}.
== Biography ==
Agatamori was born in the 7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}'s reign (668), as recorded in sources like the {{ill|Kugyō Bōnin|ja|公卿補任}}, which states he passed away in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at the age of 70.<ref name="naoki">Naoki [2006: 617]</ref> His family, the {{ill|Tajihi clan|ja|多治比氏}}, descended from {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, making them one of the prominent noble lineages of the time.<ref>Takashima [1969: 10–11]</ref>

In {{ill|Keiun 2|ja|慶雲2年}} (705), Agatamori was promoted from {{ill|Jūroku-i|ja|従六位}} to {{ill|Jūgo-i|ja|従五位下}}. His career advanced rapidly under the reign of {{ill|Empress Genmei|ja|元明天皇}}, and by {{ill|Wadō 8|ja|和銅8年}} (715), he had reached {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位下}} and was appointed {{ill|Zōkyūkyō|ja|造宮卿}}, overseeing the construction of {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}}.
In {{ill|Reiki 2|ja|霊亀2年}} (716), Agatamori was appointed Envoy to Tang China (遣唐押使). He departed for China in {{ill|Reiki 3|ja|霊亀3年}} (717), leading a large delegation of 557 members, including notable figures such as {{ill|Abe no Nakamaro|ja|阿倍仲麻呂}}, {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}, and {{ill|Gembō|ja|玄昉}}. The delegation reached {{ill|Chang'an|ja|長安}} in October and returned safely in {{ill|Yōrō 2|ja|養老2年}} (718). Agatamori received further promotions upon his return.<ref>Ueda [2006: 80–83]</ref>
=== Governor and Regional Commander ===
Agatamori was later appointed {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}} and subsequently became one of the first {{ill|Inspectors (Ancient Japan)|ja|按察使}}, overseeing the provinces of {{ill|Sagami|ja|相模}}, {{ill|Kōzuke|ja|上野}}, and {{ill|Shimotsuke|ja|下野}}. He also served as {{ill|Governor of Harima|ja|播磨守}}.
In {{ill|Yōrō 4|ja|養老4年}} (720), he was appointed {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}} to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, following the assassination of {{ill|Kamu Yamakano|ja|上毛野広人}}, the Inspector of Mutsu Province.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 45–46]</ref> His campaign achieved partial success, and he was promoted to {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位上}} upon his return.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 44]</ref>
=== Role in Court Politics ===
In {{ill|Shinki 6|ja|神亀6年}} (729), Agatamori was appointed as a provisional {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} during the {{ill|Conspiracy of Prince Nagaya|ja|長屋王の変}} and became a court noble shortly afterward, holding key positions under the {{ill|Fujiwara no Four Brothers|ja|藤原四兄弟}} regime. He reached the rank of {{ill|Shōsan-i|ja|正三位}} and served as {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言}}.
In {{ill|Tenpyō 7|ja|天平7年}} (735), he rejected a delegation from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} that had unilaterally changed its national name.<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 7, 2/27</ref>
Agatamori died in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at age 70, likely due to the smallpox epidemic that claimed many lives, including the Fujiwara brothers.<ref name="naoki" />
== Family ==
* Father: {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 9, 6/23</ref> * Children: ** Daughter: Wife of {{ill|Fujiwara no Hamanari|ja|藤原浜成}}<ref>‘Sonpi Bunmyaku’</ref> ** Daughter: Possibly {{ill|Tanbe no Iratsume|ja|丹比郎女}}, wife of {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and mother of {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}<ref>Kawakami [1971]</ref>

In {{ill|Shinki 6|ja|神亀6年}} (729), Agatamori was appointed as a provisional {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} during the {{ill|Conspiracy of Prince Nagaya|ja|長屋王の変}} and became a court noble shortly afterward, holding key positions under the {{ill|Fujiwara no Four Brothers|ja|藤原四兄弟}} regime. He reached the rank of {{ill|Shōsan-i|ja|正三位}} and served as {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言}}.
In {{ill|Tenpyō 7|ja|天平7年}} (735), he rejected a delegation from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} that had unilaterally changed its national name.<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 7, 2/27</ref>
Agatamori died in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at age 70, likely due to the smallpox epidemic that claimed many lives, including the Fujiwara brothers.<ref name="naoki" />
== Family ==
* Father: {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 9, 6/23</ref> * Children: ** Daughter: Wife of {{ill|Fujiwara no Hamanari|ja|藤原浜成}}<ref>‘Sonpi Bunmyaku’</ref> ** Daughter: Possibly {{ill|Tanbe no Iratsume|ja|丹比郎女}}, wife of {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and mother of {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}<ref>Kawakami [1971]</ref>

revision.text:
'''Tajihi no Agatamori''' (多治比縣守) was a court noble and government official during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. He was a son of {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}, who served as a {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}} (Minister of the Left). Agatamori played key roles in diplomacy, governance, and military affairs, including serving as {{ill|Envoy to Tang China|ja|遣唐使押使}}, {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}}, and {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}}.
== Biography ==
Agatamori was born in the 7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}'s reign (668), as recorded in sources like the {{ill|Kugyō Bōnin|ja|公卿補任}}, which states he passed away in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at the age of 70.<ref name="naoki">Naoki [2006: 617]</ref> His family, the {{ill|Tajihi clan|ja|多治比氏}}, descended from {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, making them one of the prominent noble lineages of the time.<ref>Takashima [1969: 10–11]</ref>
=== Mission to Tang China ===
In {{ill|Keiun 2|ja|慶雲2年}} (705), Agatamori was promoted from {{ill|Jūroku-i|ja|従六位}} to {{ill|Jūgo-i|ja|従五位下}}. His career advanced rapidly under the reign of {{ill|Empress Genmei|ja|元明天皇}}, and by {{ill|Wadō 8|ja|和銅8年}} (715), he had reached {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位下}} and was appointed {{ill|Zōkyūkyō|ja|造宮卿}}, overseeing the construction of {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}}.
In {{ill|Reiki 2|ja|霊亀2年}} (716), Agatamori was appointed Envoy to Tang China (遣唐押使). He departed for China in {{ill|Reiki 3|ja|霊亀3年}} (717), leading a large delegation of 557 members, including notable figures such as {{ill|Abe no Nakamaro|ja|阿倍仲麻呂}}, {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}, and {{ill|Gembō|ja|玄昉}}. The delegation reached {{ill|Chang'an|ja|長安}} in October and returned safely in {{ill|Yōrō 2|ja|養老2年}} (718). Agatamori received further promotions upon his return.<ref>Ueda [2006: 80–83]</ref>
=== Governor and Regional Commander ===
Agatamori was later appointed {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}} and subsequently became one of the first {{ill|Inspectors (Ancient Japan)|ja|按察使}}, overseeing the provinces of {{ill|Sagami|ja|相模}}, {{ill|Kōzuke|ja|上野}}, and {{ill|Shimotsuke|ja|下野}}. He also served as {{ill|Governor of Harima|ja|播磨守}}.
In {{ill|Yōrō 4|ja|養老4年}} (720), he was appointed {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}} to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, following the assassination of {{ill|Kamu Yamakano|ja|上毛野広人}}, the Inspector of Mutsu Province.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 45–46]</ref> His campaign achieved partial success, and he was promoted to {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位上}} upon his return.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 44]</ref>
=== Role in Court Politics ===
In {{ill|Shinki 6|ja|神亀6年}} (729), Agatamori was appointed as a provisional {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} during the {{ill|Conspiracy of Prince Nagaya|ja|長屋王の変}} and became a court noble shortly afterward, holding key positions under the {{ill|Fujiwara no Four Brothers|ja|藤原四兄弟}} regime. He reached the rank of {{ill|Shōsan-i|ja|正三位}} and served as {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言}}.
In {{ill|Tenpyō 7|ja|天平7年}} (735), he rejected a delegation from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} that had unilaterally changed its national name.<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 7, 2/27</ref>
Agatamori died in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at age 70, likely due to the smallpox epidemic that claimed many lives, including the Fujiwara brothers.<ref name="naoki" />
== Family ==
* Father: {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 9, 6/23</ref> * Children: ** Daughter: Wife of {{ill|Fujiwara no Hamanari|ja|藤原浜成}}<ref>‘Sonpi Bunmyaku’</ref> ** Daughter: Possibly {{ill|Tanbe no Iratsume|ja|丹比郎女}}, wife of {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and mother of {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}<ref>Kawakami [1971]</ref>
== References ==
{{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography ==
* Ueda, Yū. *{{ill|遣唐使全航海|ja}}*. Sōshisha, 2006. ISBN 978-4794215444. * Suzuki, Takuya. *{{ill|Emishi to Tōhoku Sensō|ja|蝦夷と東北戦争}}*. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2008. ISBN 978-4642063135. * Naoki, Kōjirō. *Nihon Kodai Chūsei Jinmei Jiten*. Yoshikawa Kōbu

In {{ill|Shinki 6|ja|神亀6年}} (729), Agatamori was appointed as a provisional {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} during the {{ill|Conspiracy of Prince Nagaya|ja|長屋王の変}} and became a court noble shortly afterward, holding key positions under the {{ill|Fujiwara no Four Brothers|ja|藤原四兄弟}} regime. He reached the rank of {{ill|Shōsan-i|ja|正三位}} and served as {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言}}.
In {{ill|Tenpyō 7|ja|天平7年}} (735), he rejected a delegation from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} that had unilaterally changed its national name.<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 7, 2/27</ref>
Agatamori died in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at age 70, likely due to the smallpox epidemic that claimed many lives, including the Fujiwara brothers.<ref name="naoki" />
== Family ==
* Father: {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 9, 6/23</ref> * Children: ** Daughter: Wife of {{ill|Fujiwara no Hamanari|ja|藤原浜成}}<ref>‘Sonpi Bunmyaku’</ref> ** Daughter: Possibly {{ill|Tanbe no Iratsume|ja|丹比郎女}}, wife of {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and mother of {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}<ref>Kawakami [1971]</ref>

'''Tajihi no Agatamori''' (多治比縣守) was a court noble and government official during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. He was a son of {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}, who served as a {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}} (Minister of the Left). Agatamori played key roles in diplomacy, governance, and military affairs, including serving as {{ill|Envoy to Tang China|ja|遣唐使押使}}, {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}}, and {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}}.
== Biography ==
Agatamori was born in the 7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}'s reign (668), as recorded in sources like the {{ill|Kugyō Bōnin|ja|公卿補任}}, which states he passed away in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at the age of 70.<ref name="naoki">Naoki [2006: 617]</ref> His family, the {{ill|Tajihi clan|ja|多治比氏}}, descended from {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, making them one of the prominent noble lineages of the time.<ref>Takashima [1969: 10–11]</ref>
=== Mission to Tang China ===
In {{ill|Keiun 2|ja|慶雲2年}} (705), Agatamori was promoted from {{ill|Jūroku-i|ja|従六位}} to {{ill|Jūgo-i|ja|従五位下}}. His career advanced rapidly under the reign of {{ill|Empress Genmei|ja|元明天皇}}, and by {{ill|Wadō 8|ja|和銅8年}} (715), he had reached {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位下}} and was appointed {{ill|Zōkyūkyō|ja|造宮卿}}, overseeing the construction of {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}}.
In {{ill|Reiki 2|ja|霊亀2年}} (716), Agatamori was appointed Envoy to Tang China (遣唐押使). He departed for China in {{ill|Reiki 3|ja|霊亀3年}} (717), leading a large delegation of 557 members, including notable figures such as {{ill|Abe no Nakamaro|ja|阿倍仲麻呂}}, {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}, and {{ill|Gembō|ja|玄昉}}. The delegation reached {{ill|Chang'an|ja|長安}} in October and returned safely in {{ill|Yōrō 2|ja|養老2年}} (718). Agatamori received further promotions upon his return.<ref>Ueda [2006: 80–83]</ref>
=== Governor and Regional Commander ===
Agatamori was later appointed {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}} and subsequently became one of the first {{ill|Inspectors (Ancient Japan)|ja|按察使}}, overseeing the provinces of {{ill|Sagami|ja|相模}}, {{ill|Kōzuke|ja|上野}}, and {{ill|Shimotsuke|ja|下野}}. He also served as {{ill|Governor of Harima|ja|播磨守}}.
In {{ill|Yōrō 4|ja|養老4年}} (720), he was appointed {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}} to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, following the assassination of {{ill|Kamu Yamakano|ja|上毛野広人}}, the Inspector of Mutsu Province.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 45–46]</ref> His campaign achieved partial success, and he was promoted to {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位上}} upon his return.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 44]</ref>
=== Role in Court Politics ===
In {{ill|Shinki 6|ja|神亀6年}} (729), Agatamori was appointed as a provisional {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} during the {{ill|Conspiracy of Prince Nagaya|ja|長屋王の変}} and became a court noble shortly afterward, holding key positions under the {{ill|Fujiwara no Four Brothers|ja|藤原四兄弟}} regime. He reached the rank of {{ill|Shōsan-i|ja|正三位}} and served as {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言}}.
In {{ill|Tenpyō 7|ja|天平7年}} (735), he rejected a delegation from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} that had unilaterally changed its national name.<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 7, 2/27</ref>
Agatamori died in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at age 70, likely due to the smallpox epidemic that claimed many lives, including the Fujiwara brothers.<ref name="naoki" />
== Family ==
* Father: {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 9, 6/23</ref> * Children: ** Daughter: Wife of {{ill|Fujiwara no Hamanari|ja|藤原浜成}}<ref>‘Sonpi Bunmyaku’</ref> ** Daughter: Possibly {{ill|Tanbe no Iratsume|ja|丹比郎女}}, wife of {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and mother of {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}<ref>Kawakami [1971]</ref>
== References ==

== Bibliography ==
* Ueda, Yū. *{{ill|遣唐使全航海|ja}}*. Sōshisha, 2006. ISBN 978-4794215444. * Suzuki, Takuya. *{{ill|Emishi to Tōhoku Sensō|ja|蝦夷と東北戦争}}*. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2008. ISBN 978-4642063135. * Naoki, Kōjirō. *Nihon Kodai Chūsei Jinmei Jiten*. Yoshikawa Kōbu

=== Role in Court Politics ===
In {{ill|Shinki 6|ja|神亀6年}} (729), Agatamori was appointed as a provisional {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} during the {{ill|Conspiracy of Prince Nagaya|ja|長屋王の変}} and became a court noble shortly afterward, holding key positions under the {{ill|Fujiwara no Four Brothers|ja|藤原四兄弟}} regime. He reached the rank of {{ill|Shōsan-i|ja|正三位}} and served as {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言}}.
In {{ill|Tenpyō 7|ja|天平7年}} (735), he rejected a delegation from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} that had unilaterally changed its national name.<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 7, 2/27</ref>
Agatamori died in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at age 70, likely due to the smallpox epidemic that claimed many lives, including the Fujiwara brothers.<ref name="naoki" />
== Family ==
* Father: {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 9, 6/23</ref> * Children: ** Daughter: Wife of {{ill|Fujiwara no Hamanari|ja|藤原浜成}}<ref>‘Sonpi Bunmyaku’</ref> ** Daughter: Possibly {{ill|Tanbe no Iratsume|ja|丹比郎女}}, wife of {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and mother of {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}<ref>Kawakami [1971]</ref>

revision.text:
'''Tajihi no Agatamori''' (多治比縣守) was a court noble and government official during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. He was a son of {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}, who served as a {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}} (Minister of the Left). Agatamori played key roles in diplomacy, governance, and military affairs, including serving as {{ill|Envoy to Tang China|ja|遣唐使押使}}, {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}}, and {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}}.
== Biography ==
Agatamori was born in the 7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}'s reign (668), as recorded in sources like the {{ill|Kugyō Bōnin|ja|公卿補任}}, which states he passed away in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at the age of 70.<ref name="naoki">Naoki [2006: 617]</ref> His family, the {{ill|Tajihi clan|ja|多治比氏}}, descended from {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, making them one of the prominent noble lineages of the time.<ref>Takashima [1969: 10–11]</ref>
=== Mission to Tang China ===
In {{ill|Keiun 2|ja|慶雲2年}} (705), Agatamori was promoted from {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位}} to {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位下}}. His career advanced rapidly under the reign of {{ill|Empress Genmei|ja|元明天皇}}, and by {{ill|Wadō 8|ja|和銅8年}} (715), he had reached {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位下}} and was appointed {{ill|Zōkyūkyō|ja|造宮卿}}, overseeing the construction of {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}}.
In {{ill|Reiki 2|ja|霊亀2年}} (716), Agatamori was appointed Envoy to Tang China (遣唐押使). He departed for China in {{ill|Reiki 3|ja|霊亀3年}} (717), leading a large delegation of 557 members, including notable figures such as {{ill|Abe no Nakamaro|ja|阿倍仲麻呂}}, {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}, and {{ill|Gembō|ja|玄昉}}. The delegation reached {{ill|Chang'an|ja|長安}} in October and returned safely in {{ill|Yōrō 2|ja|養老2年}} (718). Agatamori received further promotions upon his return.<ref>Ueda [2006: 80–83]</ref>
=== Governor and Regional Commander ===
Agatamori was later appointed {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}} and subsequently became one of the first {{ill|Inspectors (Ancient Japan)|ja|按察使}}, overseeing the provinces of {{ill|Sagami|ja|相模}}, {{ill|Kōzuke|ja|上野}}, and {{ill|Shimotsuke|ja|下野}}. He also served as {{ill|Governor of Harima|ja|播磨守}}.
In {{ill|Yōrō 4|ja|養老4年}} (720), he was appointed {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}} to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, following the assassination of {{ill|Kamu Yamakano|ja|上毛野広人}}, the Inspector of Mutsu Province.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 45–46]</ref> His campaign achieved partial success, and he was promoted to {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位上}} upon his return.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 44]</ref>
=== Role in Court Politics ===
In {{ill|Shinki 6|ja|神亀6年}} (729), Agatamori was appointed as a provisional {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} during the {{ill|Conspiracy of Prince Nagaya|ja|長屋王の変}} and became a court noble shortly afterward, holding key positions under the {{ill|Fujiwara no Four Brothers|ja|藤原四兄弟}} regime. He reached the rank of {{ill|Shōsan-i|ja|正三位}} and served as {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言}}.
In {{ill|Tenpyō 7|ja|天平7年}} (735), he rejected a delegation from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} that had unilaterally changed its national name.<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 7, 2/27</ref>
Agatamori died in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at age 70, likely due to the smallpox epidemic that claimed many lives, including the Fujiwara brothers.<ref name="naoki" />
== Family ==
* Father: {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 9, 6/23</ref> * Children: ** Daughter: Wife of {{ill|Fujiwara no Hamanari|ja|藤原浜成}}<ref>‘Sonpi Bunmyaku’</ref> ** Daughter: Possibly {{ill|Tanbe no Iratsume|ja|丹比郎女}}, wife of {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and mother of {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}<ref>Kawakami [1971]</ref>
== References ==
{{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography ==
* Ueda, Yū. *{{ill|遣唐使全航海|ja}}*. Sōshisha, 2006. ISBN 978-4794215444. * Suzuki, Takuya. *{{ill|Emishi to Tōhoku Sensō|ja|蝦夷と東北戦争}}*. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2008. ISBN 978-4642063135. * Naoki, Kōjirō. *Nihon Kodai Chūsei Jinmei Jiten*. Yoshikawa Kōbu

He departed for China in {{ill|Reiki 3|ja|霊亀3年}} (717), leading a large delegation of 557 members, including notable figures such as {{ill|Abe no Nakamaro|ja|阿倍仲麻呂}}, {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}, and {{ill|Gembō|ja|玄昉}}. The delegation reached {{ill|Chang'an|ja|長安}} in October and returned safely in {{ill|Yōrō 2|ja|養老2年}} (718). Agatamori received further promotions upon his return.<ref>Ueda [2006: 80–83]</ref>
=== Governor and Regional Commander ===
Agatamori was later appointed {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}} and subsequently became one of the first {{ill|Inspectors (Ancient Japan)|ja|按察使}}, overseeing the provinces of {{ill|Sagami|ja|相模}}, {{ill|Kōzuke|ja|上野}}, and {{ill|Shimotsuke|ja|下野}}. He also served as {{ill|Governor of Harima|ja|播磨守}}.
In {{ill|Yōrō 4|ja|養老4年}} (720), he was appointed {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}} to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, following the assassination of {{ill|Kamu Yamakano|ja|上毛野広人}}, the Inspector of Mutsu Province.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 45–46]</ref> His campaign achieved partial success, and he was promoted to {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位上}} upon his return.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 44]</ref>
=== Role in Court Politics ===
In {{ill|Shinki 6|ja|神亀6年}} (729), Agatamori was appointed as a provisional {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} during the {{ill|Conspiracy of Prince Nagaya|ja|長屋王の変}} and became a court noble shortly afterward, holding key positions under the {{ill|Fujiwara no Four Brothers|ja|藤原四兄弟}} regime. He reached the rank of {{ill|Shōsan-i|ja|正三位}} and served as {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言}}.
In {{ill|Tenpyō 7|ja|天平7年}} (735), he rejected a delegation from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} that had unilaterally changed its national name.<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 7, 2/27</ref>
Agatamori died in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at age 70, likely due to the smallpox epidemic that claimed many lives, including the Fujiwara brothers.<ref name="naoki" />

'''Tajihi no Agatamori''' (多治比縣守) was a court noble and government official during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. He was a son of {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}, who served as a {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}} (Minister of the Left). Agatamori played key roles in diplomacy, governance, and military affairs, including serving as {{ill|Envoy to Tang China|ja|遣唐使押使}}, {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}}, and {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}}.
== Biography ==
Agatamori was born in the 7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}'s reign (668), as recorded in sources like the {{ill|Kugyō Bōnin|ja|公卿補任}}, which states he passed away in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at the age of 70.<ref name="naoki">Naoki [2006: 617]</ref> His family, the {{ill|Tajihi clan|ja|多治比氏}}, descended from {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, making them one of the prominent noble lineages of the time.<ref>Takashima [1969: 10–11]</ref>

His career advanced rapidly under the reign of {{ill|Empress Genmei|ja|元明天皇}}, and by {{ill|Wadō 8|ja|和銅8年}} (715), he had reached {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位下}} and was appointed {{ill|Zōkyūkyō|ja|造宮卿}}, overseeing the construction of {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}}.
In {{ill|Reiki 2|ja|霊亀2年}} (716), Agatamori was appointed Envoy to Tang China (遣唐押使). He departed for China in {{ill|Reiki 3|ja|霊亀3年}} (717), leading a large delegation of 557 members, including notable figures such as {{ill|Abe no Nakamaro|ja|阿倍仲麻呂}}, {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}, and {{ill|Gembō|ja|玄昉}}. Agatamori was later appointed {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}} and subsequently became one of the first {{ill|Inspectors (Ancient Japan)|ja|按察使}}, overseeing the provinces of {{ill|Sagami|ja|相模}}, {{ill|Kōzuke|ja|上野}}, and {{ill|Shimotsuke|ja|下野}}. He also served as {{ill|Governor of Harima|ja|播磨守}}.
In {{ill|Yōrō 4|ja|養老4年}} (720), he was appointed {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}} to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, following the assassination of {{ill|Kamu Yamakano|ja|上毛野広人}}, the Inspector of Mutsu Province.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 45–46]</ref> His campaign achieved partial success, and he was promoted to {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位上}} upon his return.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 44]</ref>

He reached the rank of {{ill|Shōsan-i|ja|正三位}} and served as {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言}}.
In {{ill|Tenpyō 7|ja|天平7年}} (735), he rejected a delegation from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} that had unilaterally changed its national name.<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 7, 2/27</ref>
Agatamori died in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at age 70, likely due to the smallpox epidemic that claimed many lives, including the Fujiwara brothers.<ref name="naoki" />

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:trimming off this bad category]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:trimming off this bad category]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Tajihi no Agatamori''' (多治比縣守) was a court noble and government official during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. He was a son of {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}, who served as a {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}} (Minister of the Left). Agatamori played key roles in diplomacy, governance, and military affairs, including serving as {{ill|Envoy to Tang China|ja|遣唐使押使}}, {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}}, and {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}}.
== Biography ==
Agatamori was born in the 7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}'s reign (668), as recorded in sources like the {{ill|Kugyō Bōnin|ja|公卿補任}}, which states he passed away in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at the age of 70.<ref name="naoki">Naoki [2006: 617]</ref> His family, the {{ill|Tajihi clan|ja|多治比氏}}, descended from {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, making them one of the prominent noble lineages of the time.<ref>Takashima [1969: 10–11]</ref>
=== Mission to Tang China ===
In {{ill|Keiun 2|ja|慶雲2年}} (705), Agatamori was promoted from {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位}} to {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位下}}. His career advanced rapidly under the reign of {{ill|Empress Genmei|ja|元明天皇}}, and by {{ill|Wadō 8|ja|和銅8年}} (715), he had reached {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位下}} and was appointed {{ill|Zōkyūkyō|ja|造宮卿}}, overseeing the construction of {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}}.
In {{ill|Reiki 2|ja|霊亀2年}} (716), Agatamori was appointed Envoy to Tang China (遣唐押使). He departed for China in {{ill|Reiki 3|ja|霊亀3年}} (717), leading a large delegation of 557 members, including notable figures such as {{ill|Abe no Nakamaro|ja|阿倍仲麻呂}}, {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}, and {{ill|Gembō|ja|玄昉}}. The delegation reached {{ill|Chang'an|ja|長安}} in October and returned safely in {{ill|Yōrō 2|ja|養老2年}} (718). Agatamori received further promotions upon his return.<ref>Ueda [2006: 80–83]</ref>
=== Governor and Regional Commander ===
Agatamori was later appointed {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}} and subsequently became one of the first {{ill|Inspectors (Ancient Japan)|ja|按察使}}, overseeing the provinces of {{ill|Sagami|ja|相模}}, {{ill|Kōzuke|ja|上野}}, and {{ill|Shimotsuke|ja|下野}}. He also served as {{ill|Governor of Harima|ja|播磨守}}.
In {{ill|Yōrō 4|ja|養老4年}} (720), he was appointed {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}} to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, following the assassination of {{ill|Kamu Yamakano|ja|上毛野広人}}, the Inspector of Mutsu Province.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 45–46]</ref> His campaign achieved partial success, and he was promoted to {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位上}} upon his return.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 44]</ref>
=== Role in Court Politics ===
In {{ill|Shinki 6|ja|神亀6年}} (729), Agatamori was appointed as a provisional {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} during the {{ill|Conspiracy of Prince Nagaya|ja|長屋王の変}} and became a court noble shortly afterward, holding key positions under the {{ill|Fujiwara no Four Brothers|ja|藤原四兄弟}} regime. He reached the rank of {{ill|Shōsan-i|ja|正三位}} and served as {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言}}.
In {{ill|Tenpyō 7|ja|天平7年}} (735), he rejected a delegation from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} that had unilaterally changed its national name.<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 7, 2/27</ref>
Agatamori died in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at age 70, likely due to the smallpox epidemic that claimed many lives, including the Fujiwara brothers.<ref name="naoki" />
== Family ==
* Father: {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 9, 6/23</ref> * Children: ** Daughter: Wife of {{ill|Fujiwara no Hamanari|ja|藤原浜成}}<ref>‘Sonpi Bunmyaku’</ref> ** Daughter: Possibly {{ill|Tanbe no Iratsume|ja|丹比郎女}}, wife of {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and mother of {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}<ref>Kawakami [1971]</ref>
== References ==
{{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography ==
* Ueda, Yū. *{{ill|遣唐使全航海|ja}}*. Sōshisha, 2006. ISBN 978-4794215444. * Suzuki, Takuya. *{{ill|Emishi to Tōhoku Sensō|ja|蝦夷と東北戦争}}*. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2008. ISBN 978-4642063135. * Naoki, Kōjirō. *Nihon Kodai Chūsei Jinmei Jiten*. Yoshikawa Kōbu

The delegation reached {{ill|Chang'an|ja|長安}} in October and returned safely in {{ill|Yōrō 2|ja|養老2年}} (718). Agatamori received further promotions upon his return.<ref>Ueda [2006: 80–83]</ref>
=== Governor and Regional Commander ===
Agatamori was later appointed {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}} and subsequently became one of the first {{ill|Inspectors (Ancient Japan)|ja|按察使}}, overseeing the provinces of {{ill|Sagami|ja|相模}}, {{ill|Kōzuke|ja|上野}}, and {{ill|Shimotsuke|ja|下野}}. He also served as {{ill|Governor of Harima|ja|播磨守}}.
In {{ill|Yōrō 4|ja|養老4年}} (720), he was appointed {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}} to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, following the assassination of {{ill|Kamu Yamakano|ja|上毛野広人}}, the Inspector of Mutsu Province.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 45–46]</ref> His campaign achieved partial success, and he was promoted to {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位上}} upon his return.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 44]</ref>
=== Role in Court Politics ===
In {{ill|Shinki 6|ja|神亀6年}} (729), Agatamori was appointed as a provisional {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} during the {{ill|Conspiracy of Prince Nagaya|ja|長屋王の変}} and became a court noble shortly afterward, holding key positions under the {{ill|Fujiwara no Four Brothers|ja|藤原四兄弟}} regime. He reached the rank of {{ill|Shōsan-i|ja|正三位}} and served as {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言|1=Chūnagon}}.
In {{ill|Tenpyō 7|ja|天平7年}} (735), he rejected a delegation from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} that had unilaterally changed its national name.<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 7, 2/27</ref>
Agatamori died in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at age 70, likely due to the smallpox epidemic that claimed many lives, including the Fujiwara brothers.<ref name="naoki" />
== Family ==
* Father: {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 9, 6/23</ref> * Children: ** Daughter: Wife of {{ill|Fujiwara no Hamanari|ja|藤原浜成}}<ref>‘Sonpi Bunmyaku’</ref> ** Daughter: Possibly {{ill|Tanbe no Iratsume|ja|丹比郎女}}, wife of {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and mother of {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}<ref>Kawakami [1971]</ref>
== References ==
{{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography ==

revision.text:
'''Tajihi no Agatamori''' (多治比縣守) was a court noble and government official during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. He was a son of {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}, who served as a {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}} (Minister of the Left). Agatamori played key roles in diplomacy, governance, and military affairs, including serving as {{ill|Envoy to Tang China|ja|遣唐使押使}}, {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}}, and {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}}.
== Biography ==
Agatamori was born in the 7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}'s reign (668), as recorded in sources like the {{ill|Kugyō Bōnin|ja|公卿補任}}, which states he passed away in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at the age of 70.<ref name="naoki">Naoki [2006: 617]</ref> His family, the {{ill|Tajihi clan|ja|多治比氏}}, descended from {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, making them one of the prominent noble lineages of the time.<ref>Takashima [1969: 10–11]</ref>
=== Mission to Tang China ===
In {{ill|Keiun 2|ja|慶雲2年}} (705), Agatamori was promoted from {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位|1=Junior Sixth Rank}} to {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位下}}. His career advanced rapidly under the reign of {{ill|Empress Genmei|ja|元明天皇}}, and by {{ill|Wadō 8|ja|和銅8年}} (715), he had reached {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank}} and was appointed {{ill|Zōkyūkyō|ja|造宮卿}}, overseeing the construction of {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}}.
In {{ill|Reiki 2|ja|霊亀2年}} (716), Agatamori was appointed Envoy to Tang China (遣唐押使). He departed for China in {{ill|Reiki 3|ja|霊亀3年}} (717), leading a large delegation of 557 members, including notable figures such as {{ill|Abe no Nakamaro|ja|阿倍仲麻呂}}, {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}, and {{ill|Gembō|ja|玄昉}}. The delegation reached {{ill|Chang'an|ja|長安}} in October and returned safely in {{ill|Yōrō 2|ja|養老2年}} (718). Agatamori received further promotions upon his return.<ref>Ueda [2006: 80–83]</ref>
=== Governor and Regional Commander ===
Agatamori was later appointed {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}} and subsequently became one of the first {{ill|Inspectors (Ancient Japan)|ja|按察使}}, overseeing the provinces of {{ill|Sagami|ja|相模}}, {{ill|Kōzuke|ja|上野}}, and {{ill|Shimotsuke|ja|下野}}. He also served as {{ill|Governor of Harima|ja|播磨守}}.
In {{ill|Yōrō 4|ja|養老4年}} (720), he was appointed {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}} to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, following the assassination of {{ill|Kamu Yamakano|ja|上毛野広人}}, the Inspector of Mutsu Province.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 45–46]</ref> His campaign achieved partial success, and he was promoted to {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位上}} upon his return.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 44]</ref>
=== Role in Court Politics ===
In {{ill|Shinki 6|ja|神亀6年}} (729), Agatamori was appointed as a provisional {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} during the {{ill|Conspiracy of Prince Nagaya|ja|長屋王の変}} and became a court noble shortly afterward, holding key positions under the {{ill|Fujiwara no Four Brothers|ja|藤原四兄弟}} regime. He reached the rank of {{ill|Shōsan-i|ja|正三位|1=Senior Third Rank}} and served as {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言|1=Chūnagon}}.
In {{ill|Tenpyō 7|ja|天平7年}} (735), he rejected a delegation from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} that had unilaterally changed its national name.<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 7, 2/27</ref>
Agatamori died in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at age 70, likely due to the smallpox epidemic that claimed many lives, including the Fujiwara brothers.<ref name="naoki" />
== Family ==
* Father: {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 9, 6/23</ref> * Children: ** Daughter: Wife of {{ill|Fujiwara no Hamanari|ja|藤原浜成}}<ref>‘Sonpi Bunmyaku’</ref> ** Daughter: Possibly {{ill|Tanbe no Iratsume|ja|丹比郎女}}, wife of {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and mother of {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}<ref>Kawakami [1971]</ref>
== References ==
{{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography ==
* Ueda, Yū. *{{ill|遣唐使全航海|ja}}*. Sōshisha, 2006. ISBN 978-4794215444. * Suzuki, Takuya. *{{ill|Emishi to Tōhoku Sensō|ja|蝦夷と東北戦争}}*. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2008. ISBN 978-4642063135. * Naoki, Kōjirō. *Nihon Kodai Chūsei Jinmei Jiten*. Yoshikawa Kōbu

[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq-Tajihi no Agatamori-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Tajihi no Agatamori]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Tajihi no Agatamori}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq-Tajihi no Agatamori-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Tajihi no Agatamori]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq-Tajihi no Agatamori-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Tajihi no Agatamori]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Tajihi no Agatamori}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq-Tajihi no Agatamori-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Tajihi no Agatamori]][[Category:bump]] [[Category:attempting to force refresh]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tajihi no Agatamori]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Tajihi no Agatamori]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Tajihi no Agatamori]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]][[sn:Tajihi no Agatamori]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tajihi no Agatamori]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tajihi no Agatamori]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tajihi no Agatamori]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tajihi no Agatamori]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:trimming off this bad category]][[sn:Tajihi no Agatamori]][[Category:trimming off this bad category]] [[ja:多治比縣守]]

'''Tajihi no Agatamori''' (多治比縣守) was a court noble and government official during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. He was a son of {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}, who served as a {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}} (Minister of the Left). Agatamori played key roles in diplomacy, governance, and military affairs, including serving as {{ill|Envoy to Tang China|ja|遣唐使押使}}, {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}}, and {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}}.
== Biography ==
Agatamori was born in the 7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}'s reign (668), as recorded in sources like the {{ill|Kugyō Bōnin|ja|公卿補任}}, which states he passed away in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at the age of 70.<ref name="naoki">Naoki [2006: 617]</ref> His family, the {{ill|Tajihi clan|ja|多治比氏}}, descended from {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, making them one of the prominent noble lineages of the time.<ref>Takashima [1969: 10–11]</ref>
=== Mission to Tang China ===
In {{ill|Keiun 2|ja|慶雲2年}} (705), Agatamori was promoted from {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位|1=Junior Sixth Rank}} to {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位下}}. His career advanced rapidly under the reign of {{ill|Empress Genmei|ja|元明天皇}}, and by {{ill|Wadō 8|ja|和銅8年}} (715), he had reached {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank}} and was appointed {{ill|Zōkyūkyō|ja|造宮卿}}, overseeing the construction of {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}}.
In {{ill|Reiki 2|ja|霊亀2年}} (716), Agatamori was appointed Envoy to Tang China (遣唐押使). He departed for China in {{ill|Reiki 3|ja|霊亀3年}} (717), leading a large delegation of 557 members, including notable figures such as {{ill|Abe no Nakamaro|ja|阿倍仲麻呂}}, {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}, and {{ill|Gembō|ja|玄昉}}. The delegation reached {{ill|Chang'an|ja|長安}} in October and returned safely in {{ill|Yōrō 2|ja|養老2年}} (718). Agatamori received further promotions upon his return.<ref>Ueda [2006: 80–83]</ref>
=== Governor and Regional Commander ===
Agatamori was later appointed {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}} and subsequently became one of the first {{ill|Inspectors (Ancient Japan)|ja|按察使}}, overseeing the provinces of {{ill|Sagami|ja|相模}}, {{ill|Kōzuke|ja|上野}}, and {{ill|Shimotsuke|ja|下野}}. He also served as {{ill|Governor of Harima|ja|播磨守}}.
In {{ill|Yōrō 4|ja|養老4年}} (720), he was appointed {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}} to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, following the assassination of {{ill|Kamu Yamakano|ja|上毛野広人}}, the Inspector of Mutsu Province.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 45–46]</ref> His campaign achieved partial success, and he was promoted to {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位上}} upon his return.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 44]</ref>
=== Role in Court Politics ===
In {{ill|Shinki 6|ja|神亀6年}} (729), Agatamori was appointed as a provisional {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} during the {{ill|Conspiracy of Prince Nagaya|ja|長屋王の変}} and became a court noble shortly afterward, holding key positions under the {{ill|Fujiwara no Four Brothers|ja|藤原四兄弟}} regime. He reached the rank of {{ill|Shōsan-i|ja|正三位|1=Senior Third Rank}} and served as {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言|1=Chūnagon}}.
In {{ill|Tenpyō 7|ja|天平7年}} (735), he rejected a delegation from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} that had unilaterally changed its national name.<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 7, 2/27</ref>
Agatamori died in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at age 70, likely due to the smallpox epidemic that claimed many lives, including the Fujiwara brothers.<ref name="naoki" />
== Family ==
* Father: {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 9, 6/23</ref> * Children: ** Daughter: Wife of {{ill|Fujiwara no Hamanari|ja|藤原浜成}}<ref>‘Sonpi Bunmyaku’</ref> ** Daughter: Possibly {{ill|Tanbe no Iratsume|ja|丹比郎女}}, wife of {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and mother of {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}<ref>Kawakami [1971]</ref>
== References ==
{{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography ==
* Ueda, Yū. *{{ill|遣唐使全航海|ja}}*. Sōshisha, 2006. ISBN 978-4794215444. * Suzuki, Takuya. *{{ill|Emishi to Tōhoku Sensō|ja|蝦夷と東北戦争}}*. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2008. ISBN 978-4642063135. * Naoki, Kōjirō. *Nihon Kodai Chūsei Jinmei Jiten*. Yoshikawa Kōbu

He departed for China in {{ill|Reiki 3|ja|霊亀3年}} (717), leading a large delegation of 557 members, including notable figures such as {{ill|Abe no Nakamaro|ja|阿倍仲麻呂}}, {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}, and {{ill|Gembō|ja|玄昉}}. The delegation reached {{ill|Chang'an|ja|長安}} in October and returned safely in {{ill|Yōrō 2|ja|養老2年}} (718). Agatamori received further promotions upon his return.<ref>Ueda [2006: 80–83]</ref>
=== Governor and Regional Commander ===
Agatamori was later appointed {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}} and subsequently became one of the first {{ill|Inspectors (Ancient Japan)|ja|按察使}}, overseeing the provinces of {{ill|Sagami|ja|相模}}, {{ill|Kōzuke|ja|上野}}, and {{ill|Shimotsuke|ja|下野}}. He also served as {{ill|Governor of Harima|ja|播磨守}}.
In {{ill|Yōrō 4|ja|養老4年}} (720), he was appointed {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}} to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, following the assassination of {{ill|Kamu Yamakano|ja|上毛野広人}}, the Inspector of Mutsu Province.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 45–46]</ref> His campaign achieved partial success, and he was promoted to {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位上}} upon his return.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 44]</ref>
=== Role in Court Politics ===
In {{ill|Shinki 6|ja|神亀6年}} (729), Agatamori was appointed as a provisional {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} during the {{ill|Conspiracy of Prince Nagaya|ja|長屋王の変}} and became a court noble shortly afterward, holding key positions under the {{ill|Fujiwara no Four Brothers|ja|藤原四兄弟}} regime. He reached the rank of {{ill|Shōsan-i|ja|正三位|1=Senior Third Rank}} and served as {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言|1=Chūnagon}}.
In {{ill|Tenpyō 7|ja|天平7年}} (735), he rejected a delegation from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} that had unilaterally changed its national name.<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 7, 2/27</ref>
Agatamori died in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at age 70, likely due to the smallpox epidemic that claimed many lives, including the Fujiwara brothers.<ref name="naoki" />

* Father: {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 9, 6/23</ref> * Children: ** Daughter: Wife of {{ill|Fujiwara no Hamanari|ja|藤原浜成}}<ref>‘Sonpi Bunmyaku’</ref> ** Daughter: Possibly {{ill|Tanbe no Iratsume|ja|丹比郎女}}, wife of {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and mother of {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}<ref>Kawakami [1971]</ref>

revision.text:
'''Tajihi no Agatamori''' (多治比縣守) was a court noble and government official during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. He was a son of {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}, who served as a {{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}} (Minister of the Left). Agatamori played key roles in diplomacy, governance, and military affairs, including serving as {{ill|Envoy to Tang China|ja|遣唐使押使}}, {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}}, and {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}}.
== Biography ==
Agatamori was born in the 7th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}'s reign (668), as recorded in sources like the {{ill|Kugyō Bōnin|ja|公卿補任}}, which states he passed away in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at the age of 70.<ref name="naoki">Naoki [2006: 617]</ref> His family, the {{ill|Tajihi clan|ja|多治比氏}}, descended from {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, making them one of the prominent noble lineages of the time.<ref>Takashima [1969: 10–11]</ref>
=== Mission to Tang China ===
In {{ill|Keiun 2|ja|慶雲2年}} (705), Agatamori was promoted from {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位|1=Junior Sixth Rank}} to {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位下}}. His career advanced rapidly under the reign of {{ill|Empress Genmei|ja|元明天皇}}, and by {{ill|Wadō 8|ja|和銅8年}} (715), he had reached {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank}} and was appointed {{ill|Zōkyūkyō|ja|造宮卿}}, overseeing the construction of {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}}.
In {{ill|Reiki 2|ja|霊亀2年}} (716), Agatamori was appointed Envoy to Tang China (遣唐押使). He departed for China in {{ill|Reiki 3|ja|霊亀3年}} (717), leading a large delegation of 557 members, including notable figures such as {{ill|Abe no Nakamaro|ja|阿倍仲麻呂}}, {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}, and {{ill|Gembō|ja|玄昉}}. The delegation reached {{ill|Chang'an|ja|長安}} in October and returned safely in {{ill|Yōrō 2|ja|養老2年}} (718). Agatamori received further promotions upon his return.<ref>Ueda [2006: 80–83]</ref>
=== Governor and Regional Commander ===
Agatamori was later appointed {{ill|Governor of Musashi|ja|武蔵守}} and subsequently became one of the first {{ill|Inspectors (Ancient Japan)|ja|按察使}}, overseeing the provinces of {{ill|Sagami|ja|相模}}, {{ill|Kōzuke|ja|上野}}, and {{ill|Shimotsuke|ja|下野}}. He also served as {{ill|Governor of Harima|ja|播磨守}}.
In {{ill|Yōrō 4|ja|養老4年}} (720), he was appointed {{ill|Seii Taishōgun|ja|征夷大将軍}} to suppress a rebellion by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}}, following the assassination of {{ill|Kamu Yamakano|ja|上毛野広人}}, the Inspector of Mutsu Province.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 45–46]</ref> His campaign achieved partial success, and he was promoted to {{ill|Jūshi-i|ja|従四位上|1=Junior Fourth Rank|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade}} upon his return.<ref>Suzuki [2008: 44]</ref>
=== Role in Court Politics ===
In {{ill|Shinki 6|ja|神亀6年}} (729), Agatamori was appointed as a provisional {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} during the {{ill|Conspiracy of Prince Nagaya|ja|長屋王の変}} and became a court noble shortly afterward, holding key positions under the {{ill|Fujiwara no Four Brothers|ja|藤原四兄弟}} regime. He reached the rank of {{ill|Shōsan-i|ja|正三位|1=Senior Third Rank}} and served as {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言|1=Chūnagon}}.
In {{ill|Tenpyō 7|ja|天平7年}} (735), he rejected a delegation from {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}} that had unilaterally changed its national name.<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 7, 2/27</ref>
Agatamori died in {{ill|Tenpyō 9|ja|天平9年}} (737) at age 70, likely due to the smallpox epidemic that claimed many lives, including the Fujiwara brothers.<ref name="naoki" />
== Family ==
* Father: {{ill|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}<ref>‘Shoku Nihongi,’ Tenpyō 9, 6/23</ref> * Children: ** Daughter: Wife of {{ill|Fujiwara no Hamanari|ja|藤原浜成}}<ref>‘Sonpi Bunmyaku’</ref> ** Daughter: Possibly {{ill|Tanbe no Iratsume|ja|丹比郎女}}, wife of {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|ja|大伴旅人}} and mother of {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}<ref>Kawakami [1971]</ref>
== References ==
{{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography ==
* Ueda, Yū. *{{ill|遣唐使全航海|ja}}*. Sōshisha, 2006. ISBN 978-4794215444. * Suzuki, Takuya. *{{ill|Emishi to Tōhoku Sensō|ja|蝦夷と東北戦争}}*. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2008. ISBN 978-4642063135. * Naoki, Kōjirō. *Nihon Kodai Chūsei Jinmei Jiten*. Yoshikawa Kōbu
